claim: Unsanitized paneId interpolated into a CSS attribute selector passed to document.querySelector; malformed pane IDs from crafted workspace files can cause a DOMException (popover silently fails to render) or unintended element selection (popover anchors at wrong coordinates).
evidence: Line 36 of the new SplitMenu.tsx: `document.querySelector(`[data-testid="split-${paneId}"]`)`. The paneId prop originates from the Mosaic layout tree, which is populated from deserialized workspace data. deserializeWorkspace in src/shared/workspace-model.ts (lines 103-115) validates `typeof ws.panes !== 'object'` but does NOT validate that individual pane-ID keys conform to the UUID format; any string key is accepted. A crafted workspace file with a pane ID containing CSS attribute-selector metacharacters (e.g. a double-quote `"` splits the attribute value and produces an invalid selector like `[data-testid="split-evil" extra]`) causes querySelector to throw a DOMException. Because no try/catch wraps the useLayoutEffect callback, the exception propagates to React's error boundary and `pos` stays null, so the split popover never appears. This is a new code path introduced by this feature: the prior SplitMenu was positioned absolutely inside the tile and never queried the DOM by paneId.
recommendation: Sanitize `paneId` before interpolating it into the CSS selector, or avoid the querySelector pattern entirely. Preferred: use a React ref on the toolbar split button and pass it down to SplitMenu via a prop or a RefObject stored in a context, eliminating the DOM query. If document.querySelector is retained, validate the paneId matches `/^[0-9a-f-]+$/i` (UUID format) before using it in the selector; fall back to a safe default position (e.g. center-screen) when validation fails. Additionally, harden deserializeWorkspace to validate pane ID keys as UUID strings so the invariant is enforced at ingestion time rather than at each use site.

claim: The `dir='row'` argument passed to addTerminal/addEditor/addExplorer in commit() is dead at this call site: the store silently discards it whenever splitDir is provided.
evidence: commit() always calls e.g. addTerminal(wsId, paneId, 'row', dir) where dir is a SplitDir4. The store implementation is `const lay = splitDir ? splitDirToLayout(splitDir) : { direction: dir, position: 'after' }`, so the positional 'row' is unreachable whenever splitDir is set — which SplitMenu always does. A maintainer changing 'row' to 'column' here would have no observable effect and no compiler error.
recommendation: Remove the dual-mode interface ambiguity. The simplest fix is to drop the required `dir: MosaicDirection` parameter from addTerminal/addEditor/addExplorer when a SplitDir4 covers the intent — make dir optional (defaulting to 'row') so call sites that genuinely need MosaicDirection still work, and SplitMenu can omit it entirely: addTerminal(wsId, paneId, undefined, dir). Alternatively, document the dummy value with an inline comment at each call site so the override is obvious to the next reader.

claim: `dirRefs` is typed as `Record<string, HTMLButtonElement | null>` instead of `Partial<Record<SplitDir4, HTMLButtonElement | null>>`, losing compile-time key-type enforcement.
evidence: Line 32: `const dirRefs = useRef<Record<string, HTMLButtonElement | null>>({})`. Accesses like `dirRefs.current['right']` and `dirRefs.current[d]` (where d is SplitDir4) are unchecked: a typo such as 'righ' silently produces undefined and the subsequent `?.focus()` no-ops without any TypeScript error.
recommendation: Type the ref as `useRef<Partial<Record<SplitDir4, HTMLButtonElement | null>>>({})`. This makes the compiler verify that only SplitDir4 keys are stored and looked up, surfacing any key mismatch at the write site (ref assignment) and read site (focus call).

claim: Roving tabindex is only half-implemented: `tabIndex` attributes are not updated when arrow-key navigation moves focus, so the Tab-away-then-back re-entry point is always 'right' instead of the last active direction.
evidence: dirButton() sets tabIndex statically: `tabIndex={d === 'right' ? 0 : -1}`. The onKeyDown handler calls `dirRefs.current[d]?.focus()` but never updates tabIndex on the newly focused button. After pressing ArrowDown (focus now on 'down', tabIndex still -1) and then Tab (leaves group), Tab-back returns to 'right' (the only tabIndex=0 button). The full roving-tabindex pattern requires setting tabIndex=0 on the incoming element and -1 on the outgoing one so that re-entry restores the last position. REQ-010 names the pattern explicitly.
recommendation: Track the active direction in a ref (e.g. `activeDir`). In onKeyDown, after calling focus(), also set `dirRefs.current[prev]?.setAttribute('tabindex', '-1')` and `dirRefs.current[d]?.setAttribute('tabindex', '0')` (or drive it via state and re-render). A simpler alternative is to omit the tabIndex entirely from non-active buttons and use a single tabIndex=0 on the active one, managed via state.

claim: When kind='explorer' and cwd is falsy, commit() silently closes the popover without performing a split — an undefended invariant.
evidence: commit() branches: `if (kind === 'terminal') ... else if (kind === 'editor') ... else if (cwd) addExplorer(...)`. The explorer branch falls through when cwd is falsy; onClose() is called unconditionally, so the menu closes with no action. The disabled prop on the Explorer kind button prevents reaching this state via normal UI, but commit() has no guard — a future code path that calls commit() with kind='explorer' and no cwd (e.g., cwd clearing between kind-select and direction-click in a race) would silently swallow the action.
recommendation: Add an early-return guard: `if (kind === 'explorer' && !cwd) return` before the onClose() call, or an assertion comment explaining the invariant. Alternatively, disable direction buttons when kind='explorer' and !cwd, making the invariant enforced at the interaction layer.

claim: Production positioning logic is coupled to the testid string 'split-${paneId}' via a raw document.querySelector call, creating a hidden dependency between the split menu's render position and the testid contract.
evidence: useLayoutEffect: `document.querySelector('[data-testid="split-${paneId}"]')`. Testids are test-infrastructure by convention; here they double as a production anchor mechanism. If the testid is renamed (e.g. in a refactor or after a split-button redesign), the popover silently renders at null and returns early (`if (!pos) return null`), with no error surfaced to the developer. The coupling also makes it impossible to render SplitMenu without the testid being present in the DOM.
recommendation: Pass the anchor's position from the parent as a prop — either a `DOMRect` or a React ref. PaneTile already controls the toggle() that opens the menu; it can forward the split button's ref to SplitMenu. This removes the testid dependency from production code and makes the positioning relationship explicit in the component API.

claim: Moving Record out of the always-visible toolbar (REQ-001/002) silently deleted the ONLY ambient indicator that a terminal is actively recording. The old toolbar button rendered a persistent red glyph (`style={{ color: recording ? '#ff6b6b' : undefined }}` on `⏺`), so a running recording was visible at all times. The new design exposes recording state ONLY inside the right-click context menu label ('Stop recording' vs 'Start recording'): a session that is recording — a background process that accumulates files on disk — now has zero on-screen cue. A user can leave a terminal recording indefinitely with no way to notice short of right-clicking each pane. The spec treated this as a pure relocation ('only its location changes', REQ-002 / concept decision 1) and never required a replacement ongoing-state indicator. This is the classic 'missing requirement' the spec ignored: feedback for a persistent, resource/disk-consuming background state was dropped, not merely relocated.
evidence: git HEAD:PaneToolbar.tsx shows `<button data-testid={`rec-${paneId}`} title={recording ? 'Stop recording' : 'Record session'} style={{ color: recording ? '#ff6b6b' : undefined }} ...>⏺</button>` — a colored, state-reflecting toolbar control. The working-tree PaneToolbar has no recording element at all, and TEST-019 only round-trips state through the hidden context-menu label; no test/REQ requires a visible recording indicator. PaneTile's `chipText`/status chrome shows process/AI/git but never recording.
recommendation: Add an unobtrusive but persistent recording indicator (e.g. a small red dot on the pane title bar / proc chip, or a `data-recording` attribute styled paint-only per REQ-012) so an active recording is discoverable at a glance, restoring the ambient feedback the toolbar button provided. File as a spec gap: REQ-001/002 should mandate a replacement indicator for the ongoing recording state, not just relocate the toggle.

claim: The compass popover is anchored at `top: r.bottom + 4` with NO vertical viewport clamp or upward flip (only the horizontal axis is guarded by `Math.max(4, ...)`). The popover is tall (3x28 compass grid + 3 stacked kind buttons + padding/gaps ≈ 170-190px). For any pane whose toolbar sits in the bottom row of the mosaic — the common case for a vertically-split or bottom-row pane — the split button's `r.bottom` is near the viewport bottom, so the popover renders off the bottom edge and the kind selector (and lower compass arrows) are clipped/unclickable by mouse. Unlike a click-anchored menu (PaneContextMenu uses the cursor coords), this popover is deterministically anchored to a fixed toolbar element that is always at the tile's top edge, so a bottom-row pane reliably opens the menu off-screen. REQ-004 only asserts the portal escapes the tile's clip; it never requires the popover to stay within the viewport, so no test catches this. This is exactly the 'surprising/lost geometry' case the lens hunts — here the lost geometry is the popover itself.
evidence: `const r = btn?.getBoundingClientRect(); if (r) setPos({ left: Math.max(4, r.right - 168), top: r.bottom + 4 })` — `top` has no `Math.min` against `window.innerHeight - popoverHeight` and no flip-above-the-button fallback. e2e tests open the popover only on the top/first pane, so the bottom-row clip is never exercised.
recommendation: Clamp/flip the vertical anchor: if `r.bottom + estimatedHeight > window.innerHeight`, render above the button (`top: r.top - height - 4`) or clamp `top` into the viewport; add an e2e that opens the compass on a bottom-row pane (after a `down` split) and asserts the `split-menu` bounding box is fully within the viewport.
resolution: Resolved (iteration 2, verified by devils-advocate re-review). Anchor math flips the popover above the trigger (r.top-EST_H-4) when it would overflow below and clamps both axes into the viewport (SplitMenu.tsx:53-58); full popover stays on-screen for bottom-row panes, never off the top.

claim: The combined-surface design forces one keydown handler to serve two controls with conflicting keyboard models, and the implementation resolves the conflict by globally hijacking the arrow keys for the compass. `onKeyDown` is bound to the whole `split-menu` container and maps EVERY ArrowUp/Down/Left/Right to `dirRefs.current[d].focus()` with `e.preventDefault()`. Consequence: when focus is on a `split-kind-*` radio (the kind selector is `role=radiogroup` with `role=radio` children), pressing an arrow key — the conventional, screen-reader-expected way to change a radio selection — does NOT move between kind options; it yanks focus out of the radiogroup onto a compass arrow. So the kind selector cannot be navigated by arrow keys at all; a user/AT that follows the radiogroup contract is silently broken and the selection can only be changed via Tab + Space. REQ-010's 'the kind selector MUST also be reachable/changeable by keyboard' is only marginally satisfied (Space works), but the design actively violates the ARIA radiogroup interaction it advertises via `role=radio`/`aria-checked`. The core assumption — that a spatial compass and a linear radiogroup can share one arrow-key space on one surface — is the flaw.
evidence: `const map = { ArrowUp:'up', ArrowDown:'down', ArrowLeft:'left', ArrowRight:'right' }; const d = map[e.key]; if (d) { e.preventDefault(); dirRefs.current[d]?.focus() }` on the container `<div data-testid="split-menu" onKeyDown={onKeyDown}>`, which also contains the `role=radiogroup` kind buttons. No branch checks whether the event target is a kind radio before consuming the arrow.
recommendation: Scope the arrow handling to the compass group only (attach onKeyDown to the compass `role=group` element, or early-return when `e.target` is a kind radio so the radiogroup keeps native arrow semantics), and give the radiogroup proper roving arrow navigation. Or revisit the single-surface assumption.

claim: The popover's keyboard story (REQ-010) ignores focus containment and focus return, and the implementation has no focus trap. On open it `.focus()`es the right arrow, but: (1) there is no focus trap, and the only tabbable elements are the right arrow (tabIndex 0; the other three arrows are -1) plus the three kind radios — so a single Tab past the explorer radio moves focus OUT of the portalled popover to whatever follows `document.body`, landing the user behind the still-open overlay; (2) once focus has left the popover, the Esc handler (bound to the `split-menu` div) no longer receives the keydown, so Esc fails to dismiss — the popover is stuck open with focus lost behind it; (3) on Esc-dismiss `onClose()` only sets `menu=null` and never returns focus to the `split-${paneId}` trigger, dropping keyboard focus to `<body>`. Commit happens to refocus the new pane via `requestPaneFocus`, but the cancel path strands the keyboard user. REQ-010 specifies open-focus, arrows, Enter, Esc but never specifies focus trapping or return-focus — the case the spec ignored.
evidence: `useEffect(() => { dirRefs.current['right']?.focus() }, [pos])` for open-focus; `onClose` in PaneTile is `() => setMenu(null)` with no focus restoration; the popover root has no focus-trap / `onKeyDown` Tab handling; Escape is handled only inside `onKeyDown` on the `split-menu` div, so it is unreachable once focus tabs out.
recommendation: Trap focus within the popover (cycle Tab/Shift-Tab among the compass + kind controls) or listen for Escape at the document level, and on close (both commit and Esc) return focus to the `split-${paneId}` button. Add the missing focus-management requirement to REQ-010 and an e2e asserting focus returns to the trigger after Esc.
resolution: Resolved (iteration 2, verified by devils-advocate re-review). Tab/Shift-Tab trapped among enabled buttons (SplitMenu.tsx:78-91); Esc closes from any child; close() returns focus to the split-${paneId} trigger on every path (Esc/backdrop/commit) (SplitMenu.tsx:67); trigger gains aria-haspopup=dialog + aria-expanded (PaneToolbar.tsx:56-57 via PaneTile.tsx:97).

claim: REQ-007's MUST that 'up/down ⇒ column' (a vertical/stacked split) is never pinned end-to-end by an observable assertion. TEST-011 exercises right (editor), TEST-012 pins left with before-insertion DOM order, TEST-014 presses ArrowDown then Enter but only asserts the tile count rises to 2 — it never asserts the resulting parent is a `column` (vertical) split or the stacked DOM order; TEST-022 selects 'a downward direction' but asserts only that an explorer pane opens rooted at the cwd, not the orientation. The column orientation for up/down is therefore covered ONLY by the pure mapping unit test (TEST-005). Because the store derives geometry exclusively through `splitDirToLayout`, a regression that fed the wrong orientation in the UI commit path (e.g. SplitMenu passing the literal `'row'` if the `splitDir` override were ever dropped — see the dead-param risk in FINDING-QOL-001) would still pass every e2e: the suite proves a column mapping exists in isolation but never proves the compass actually produces a column split on screen.
evidence: Test map: no e2e asserts a `column` MosaicDirection / vertically-stacked tile geometry resulting from an up or down compass activation; only TEST-005 (pure) covers column, and TEST-014/022 assert count/kind/cwd, not orientation.
recommendation: Add an e2e that activates `split-dir-down`/`split-dir-up` and asserts the new pane is stacked vertically (column geometry / DOM order or the persisted layout `direction === 'column'`), mirroring TEST-012's before-insertion DOM-order check for the horizontal axis.

claim: The compass has no visible highlight for the focused/default/hovered direction. REQ-010 requires the right ▶ target to be the 'initial highlighted target' on open, but the only highlight is DOM focus via dirRefs.current['right'].focus(). On a mouse-opened popover that programmatic focus does NOT match :focus-visible in Chromium, so no UA focus ring appears; and the app's deliberate accent focus ring (index.css:187) and hover highlight (index.css:139) are scoped to an allow-list of chrome data-testids that excludes the new 'split-menu' surface (which is also portalled to <body>, outside .mosaic-window-toolbar). Net: a mouse user opening the popover sees zero indication which direction is the default/will-commit-on-Enter target, and hovering a direction gives no feedback at all (concept decision 4: 'hover/focus highlights the target').
evidence: SplitMenu.tsx:42 `useEffect(() => { dirRefs.current['right']?.focus() }, [pos])` is the sole highlight mechanism; dirButton (60-66) applies only static PAINT with no :focus/:hover (inline styles cannot express pseudo-classes). index.css:132-190 :focus-visible/:hover/:active rules enumerate workspace-tabs/status-bar/mosaic-window-toolbar/...cwd-menu/cloud-menu but never 'split-menu'. No global outline reset exists, so keyboard nav shows only the plain UA ring (inconsistent with the rest of the chrome), and mouse-open shows nothing.
recommendation: Give the direction targets an explicit highlight state driven by a tracked 'focused direction' (e.g. data-active + a paint-only background/ring class) rather than relying on browser :focus-visible, and add a :hover paint highlight; OR add 'split-menu' / the split-dir testids to the index.css focus-visible + hover allow-lists so the popover matches the rest of the chrome. Ensure the default 'right' target is visibly highlighted immediately on open regardless of pointer-vs-keyboard. propose CONV: any new chrome popover/menu portalled to <body> must be added to the focus-visible and hover style allow-lists (or carry equivalent paint-only focus/hover styling) so keyboard focus and hover are always visible.
resolution: Resolved (iteration 2, verified by ux re-review). SplitMenu now tracks an active direction (default right) painting a distinct accent background+ring independent of :focus-visible (SplitMenu.tsx:40,100-113); split-menu added to index.css hover + focus-visible allow-lists (index.css:139,190), paint-only/box-neutral. Visible on mouse-open.

claim: The kind selector is built as an ARIA radiogroup (role=radiogroup / role=radio / aria-checked) but does not implement the radio keyboard model, so the role's promise is broken. The popover container's onKeyDown (51-58) intercepts ALL arrow keys with e.preventDefault() and moves focus to a compass direction button, so a screen-reader user who Tabs to the kind radios and presses Arrow keys (the standard, announced way to change a radio selection) cannot change the kind and instead has focus yanked into the compass. The three radios are also each native buttons (default tabIndex 0), making three tab stops instead of the single roving tab stop a radiogroup must have. Result: AT announces 'radio button, 1 of 3' and offers arrow selection that silently does nothing.
evidence: onKeyDown maps ArrowUp/Down/Left/Right unconditionally to dirRefs focus regardless of whether focus is in the compass or the kind group; kindButton (68-75) renders role=radio without managing roving tabindex among the radios and selection only happens via onClick (mouse/Space/Enter), never via arrows.
recommendation: Scope the arrow-key handler to the compass only (e.g. attach onKeyDown to the role=group compass container, not the whole popover), and implement proper radiogroup semantics for the kind selector: a single roving tabindex across the three radios with Arrow keys moving/changing the checked radio. Alternatively drop role=radio and use aria-pressed buttons (REQ-011 permits this) so no arrow-key contract is implied.
resolution: Resolved (iteration 2, verified by ux re-review). Kind controls converted from broken role=radiogroup to aria-pressed toggle-buttons in role=group; arrow keys scoped to the compass only so kind buttons keep native Tab/Space (SplitMenu.tsx:95-101,117-145). TEST-009/016 green.

claim: Dismissing the popover (Esc, backdrop click, or after a commit) does not restore focus to the split button that opened it. onClose() just sets the PaneTile menu state to null; focus falls to <body>. A keyboard or screen-reader user who opens the compass and presses Esc is left with no focus context and must re-traverse the toolbar to find their place, a standard popover focus-management failure.
evidence: onKeyDown Escape handler (52) calls onClose() with no focus return; the split button (`split-${paneId}`) is never re-focused. No ref to the trigger is captured.
recommendation: Capture the trigger element (or its testid) and restore focus to the `split-${paneId}` button on close/Esc/commit, mirroring standard dialog/popover behavior.

claim: The split trigger button exposes no popup semantics: it has no aria-haspopup (the popover is a dialog/menu) and no aria-expanded reflecting open/closed. A screen-reader user is told only 'Split (compass...), button' and gets no signal that activating it opens a popover or that the popover is currently open.
evidence: `<button data-testid={`split-${paneId}`} title="Split (compass: up / left / right / down)" onClick={() => toggle('split')}>` carries no aria-haspopup / aria-expanded; the toolbar tracks open state in PaneTile `menu === 'split'` but never reflects it on the trigger.
recommendation: Add aria-haspopup="dialog" (or "menu") and aria-expanded={menu === 'split'} to the split button, wiring the open state down from PaneTile.

claim: The Explorer kind option is disabled when the source pane has no cwd, but nothing explains why. There is no title/aria-describedby telling the user 'no working directory yet', so a user sees a greyed Explorer with no reason and no path to enable it. The disabled state is also weakly conveyed: the chrome :disabled dimming rule (index.css:160) is scoped to an allow-list that excludes 'split-menu', so the disabled Explorer is only distinguished by its unselected opacity (0.7) — visually indistinguishable from a normal enabled-but-unselected kind.
evidence: kindButton sets `disabled={k === 'explorer' && !cwd}` with no title/aria-describedby; index.css:153-163 :disabled opacity rule does not list split-menu, and the inline style only varies opacity by selection, not by disabled.
recommendation: Add an explanatory title/aria-describedby on the disabled Explorer option (e.g. 'Explorer needs a working directory — none reported yet') and give disabled kind options a clearly distinct (dimmer, not-allowed cursor) paint state, e.g. by extending the chrome :disabled allow-list to split-menu.

claim: Moving Record entirely into the right-click context menu removes the only at-a-glance indicator that a terminal pane is actively recording. The old toolbar ⏺ toggle reflected `recording` state in the always-visible bar; now nothing in the toolbar or title bar signals an in-progress recording. A user who started recording (or returned to a tab later) cannot tell a pane is recording without right-clicking it, and may leave recordings running unknowingly. The spec only specified relocating the control and never required a persistent active-recording indicator.
evidence: PaneToolbar no longer renders any rec-* element (REQ-001); the new control is `pane-menu-record` inside PaneContextMenu, surfaced only on right-click. PaneTile's title (line 56-57) reflects needs-input with a 🔔 prefix but has no equivalent for `recording`.
recommendation: Add a small always-visible recording indicator (e.g. a red dot in the title bar or a status chip) bound to `recording[paneId]` so active recording is discoverable without opening the context menu; or note in the title like the needs-input bell prefix.

claim: The compass is not a fully correct roving-tabindex widget: tabIndex is hardcoded so 'right' is always the single tab stop (tabIndex={d === 'right' ? 0 : -1}) and is never updated as the user arrows to another direction. After arrowing to e.g. 'down' then Tab-ing out to the kind group and Shift-Tab-ing back, focus returns to 'right' rather than the last-focused direction — a deviation from the roving-tabindex pattern the component's own comment claims to implement.
evidence: dirButton sets a static tabIndex by direction; onKeyDown moves focus with .focus() but never updates which target holds tabIndex 0, and there is no state tracking the active direction.
recommendation: Track the active direction in state and set tabIndex={d === active ? 0 : -1}, updating it on arrow navigation so the tab stop follows the focused target (true roving tabindex).

claim: The popover is not focus-trapped and the container div carries no dialog/region role or aria-label. Although the inner compass (role=group) and kind (role=radiogroup) are labelled, the surface itself is an unnamed div, and Tab from the last kind radio escapes into the app obscured behind the click-catcher backdrop (the backdrop captures clicks but not focus) while the popover stays open — a keyboard user can interact with hidden controls.
evidence: split-menu div (82) has no role/aria-label; backdrop (80-81) is click-only; there is no focus trap or onBlur-close, so Shift+Tab/Tab can move focus outside the open popover.
recommendation: Add role="dialog" + aria-label="Split pane" (or aria-labelledby) to the surface and trap focus within the open popover (or close it on focus-out), so keyboard focus cannot reach the obscured app while the compass is open.

claim: The selected-kind indicator is weak for a control whose stated goal is 'modern and sleek, not basic UX'. The chosen kind is conveyed only by fontWeight 700 and an opacity difference (1 vs 0.7), with no background fill, accent border, or check affordance. Selected vs unselected is easy to miss at a glance, and the default Terminal selection is therefore not prominently visible.
evidence: kindButton style: `fontWeight: kind === k ? 700 : 400, opacity: kind === k ? 1 : 0.7` over a transparent PAINT background; no selected background/accent treatment.
recommendation: Give the selected kind a clear paint-only selected state (accent background/border) in addition to weight, so the default Terminal selection and any change reads clearly.

claim: The split call chain now carries TWO direction parameters for one concept: the legacy `dir: MosaicDirection` and the new `splitDir?: SplitDir4`, where `splitDir` silently overrides `dir` (store: `const lay = splitDir ? splitDirToLayout(splitDir) : { direction: dir, position: 'after' }`). After this change EVERY call site hardcodes `'row'` for `dir` (SplitMenu passes `addTerminal(wsId, paneId, 'row', dir)`; WorkspaceView, dispatchAddPane, openExplorerHere all pass `'row'`), so `dir` is now effectively a dead/vestigial param while the real direction rides `splitDir`. A maintainer reading `addTerminal(wsId, paneId, 'row', dir)` is actively misled: the literal `'row'` looks load-bearing but is ignored whenever the 4th arg is set, and the local var is even named `dir` (a SplitDir4) colliding with the function's MosaicDirection `dir`. Passing a meaningful `dir='column'` together with `splitDir='right'` would silently drop the column with no warning.
evidence: Grep shows all 9 add* call sites pass `'row'` as the MosaicDirection arg; only `splitDir` ever varies. `splitDir` fully subsumes `dir` (splitDirToLayout returns both direction AND position), so `dir` could be deleted from addTerminal/addEditor/addExplorer/commitPane/placePane in favor of threading `splitDir` (or `{direction,position}`) alone.
fix: Collapse the redundant param: thread a single `splitDir?: SplitDir4` (defaulting to 'right'/after) or a single `{direction, position}` through addTerminal/addEditor/addExplorer/commitPane/placePane and drop the now-always-'row' MosaicDirection arg; or at minimum rename the SplitMenu local so it doesn't shadow `dir` and document the override precedence at each signature. propose CONV: a function MUST NOT expose two parameters that encode the same concept where one silently overrides the other; thread one canonical value instead of a primary+override pair.

claim: The single combined split button still renders the old `⬌` (left-right double-arrow) glyph, which signals a horizontal-only split and undercuts discoverability of the new four-way compass (up/left/right/down + before-insertion). The capability grew but the affordance icon still says 'horizontal split'. The tooltip text was updated ('Split (compass: up / left / right / down)') but the visible glyph — the only thing a user sees before hovering — was not.
evidence: PaneToolbar split button: `data-testid={`split-${paneId}`} title="Split (compass: up / left / right / down)" ...>⬌`. The popover now offers 4 directions and a kind selector; the glyph implies only the old right-split.
fix: Use a glyph that reads as multi-directional / 'split menu' (e.g. ✛, ⊞, ⊹, or a compass/plus icon) so the four-way capability is discoverable at a glance, not only on hover. Keep it paint-only per REQ-012.

claim: Popover horizontal anchoring uses a hardcoded magic offset `r.right - 168` while the popover itself declares `minWidth: 156` and padding 6. The `168` is not derived from the rendered width, so any future restyle of the popover width silently mis-anchors it relative to the split button (drift to the left/right of the trigger). This is a debuggability/maintainability footgun: the relationship between the anchor math and the box dimensions is implicit.
evidence: `if (r) setPos({ left: Math.max(4, r.right - 168), top: r.bottom + 4 })` with the surface styled `minWidth: 156, padding: 6`. 168 != 156 and is unexplained.
fix: Derive the offset from the popover's measured width (or a shared named constant referenced by both the anchor math and the style), and add a brief comment tying 168 to the intended width so a restyle stays aligned.

claim: `splitDirToLayout` is a bare `switch` over the four SplitDir4 cases with no `default` branch. It is type-safe at the typed boundary, but if an out-of-type value ever reaches it (untyped IPC/persisted/test input) the function returns `undefined`, and the caller's `lay.direction` / `lay.position` access then throws a cryptic `Cannot read properties of undefined`, with no value named in the error (CONV-001 — specific/actionable errors). The direction plumbing has no debug breadcrumb identifying the offending input.
evidence: switch(dir){ case 'right'...case 'up'... } with no default; callers immediately destructure `lay.direction`/`lay.position`.
fix: Add a `default` that throws naming the bad value (e.g. `throw new Error(`splitDirToLayout: unknown direction '${dir}'`)`) or an exhaustiveness `assertNever`, so a bad direction fails loudly and debuggably instead of producing a downstream undefined dereference.

claim: REQ-014 cites TEST-025 in traceability.json (and 04-tests.md describes it as the loop-back reconciliation marker), but no test file carries a `TEST-025` id. A repo-wide search for `TEST-025` finds it only inside this feature's own docs, never in any test name/comment. This contradicts 04-tests.md's own contract: "Each test carries its `TEST-NNN` id in its describe/test name so the Gatekeeper can confirm it exists." The reconciliation it names (smoke/broadcast/ui-polish/notepad/persistence/run-commands/workspace-templates via tests/e2e/split-helper.ts, and recording via pane-menu-record) genuinely happened, but is unlabeled, so the cited test id is unverifiable.
evidence: grep -rEoh 'TEST-[0-9]{3}' tests/ yields TEST-001..TEST-024 only (no TEST-025). traceability.json REQ-014.tests = [TEST-021,TEST-022,TEST-023,TEST-025]. 04-tests.md lines 13-17, 47, 77 reference TEST-025. The reconciled specs use split-helper.ts (confirmed) but none contain a TEST-025 marker. REQ-014 itself remains covered by the real TEST-021/022/023 markers.
recommendation: Either add a `TEST-025` marker (e.g. in tests/e2e/split-helper.ts's leading comment and/or one reconciled spec) so the cited id is machine-discoverable, or drop TEST-025 from traceability.json REQ-014.tests and reword 04-tests.md to record the reconciliation as prose under the existing TEST ids rather than a phantom test id.
resolution: Reconciled in doc-sync (phase 7): traceability/04-tests corrected (phantom TEST-025 dropped, REQ-008 cites split-direction.test.ts), workspaces.md inventory + CLAUDE.md portal gotcha updated.

claim: REQ-008's acceptance criteria name `tests/shared/workspace-model.test.ts` as the home of the splitPane before/after default-after pins and the splitDirToLayout mapping test, but those unit tests were actually authored in `tests/shared/split-direction.test.ts` (17 splitPane/splitDirToLayout references). workspace-model.test.ts contains zero `position`/`before`/`splitDirToLayout` assertions. The cited file does not host the cited tests.
evidence: grep -c 'splitPane|splitDirToLayout' tests/shared/split-direction.test.ts = 17; grep for 'position|before|splitDirToLayout' in tests/shared/workspace-model.test.ts = no matches. 04-tests.md line 41 places TEST-001..005 in split-direction.test.ts. The REQ is still covered, only the spec's file pointer is stale.
recommendation: At doc-sync, update REQ-008's acceptance text to point at tests/shared/split-direction.test.ts (where TEST-001..005 live), or move/duplicate the assertions into workspace-model.test.ts to match the spec.
resolution: Reconciled in doc-sync (phase 7): traceability/04-tests corrected (phantom TEST-025 dropped, REQ-008 cites split-direction.test.ts), workspaces.md inventory + CLAUDE.md portal gotcha updated.

claim: The workspaces.md test inventory still lists only `tests/shared/workspace-model.test.ts` for layout-op coverage; the feature's new pure test file `tests/shared/split-direction.test.ts` (the splitPane before/after + splitDirToLayout coverage, TEST-001..005) is not added to the doc's test list, even though the prose above it now documents the new splitDirToLayout helper and the before/after position parameter.
evidence: grep for 'split-direction' in docs/features/workspaces.md = no matches, while the same doc (line 19, edited by this feature) now describes splitDirToLayout. The Tests section (line 135) names workspace-model.test.ts but omits the new sibling test file.
recommendation: Add a one-line entry for tests/shared/split-direction.test.ts to the workspaces.md Tests inventory so the doc's test list matches the shipped tests.
resolution: Reconciled in doc-sync (phase 7): traceability/04-tests corrected (phantom TEST-025 dropped, REQ-008 cites split-direction.test.ts), workspaces.md inventory + CLAUDE.md portal gotcha updated.

claim: The load-bearing "Overlays opened from inside a mosaic tile must portal to <body>" gotcha lists only `PaneContextMenu` and `Modal` as the components that `createPortal` to escape the tile's transform/clip. As of this feature `SplitMenu.tsx` (the combined split/compass popover) is now ALSO a fixed-position child of a mosaic tile that portals to document.body for exactly this reason, but it is not named in the gotcha. A future maintainer reading the gotcha would not learn that the split popover relies on the same pattern.
evidence: SplitMenu.tsx now ends with `createPortal(..., document.body)` and its own doc-comment cites the tile-transform clipping rationale; CLAUDE.md lines 119-121 enumerate only PaneContextMenu and Modal as the escapees. REQ-004 made the split popover a portalled overlay. Note: CLAUDE.md has NO stale rec-/split-col- testid references (grep clean), so it needed no removals — only this additive mention is missing.
recommendation: Add SplitMenu to the CLAUDE.md portal gotcha (e.g. "PaneContextMenu, Modal, and SplitMenu createPortal to escape it") at doc-sync.
resolution: Reconciled in doc-sync (phase 7): traceability/04-tests corrected (phantom TEST-025 dropped, REQ-008 cites split-direction.test.ts), workspaces.md inventory + CLAUDE.md portal gotcha updated.
